In my opinion, this place is a bit overrated. The complex is interesting, but the climb up the thousand steps is tedious and probably not worth the rather large sums of money that locals charge tourists. I definitely liked the nearby Pidurangala better, where the entrance fee was ridiculously cheap, the route to the top was much more interesting (but also challenging – boulders, rocks, crevices, etc.), and the view from the top was equally impressive, but it offered more space and space to admire the sunset or simply relax.

The sunrise is very good to watch from here. However, the entrance fee is very high at $30 per person. If you're not specifically interested in the ruins, you should watch the sunrise and/or sunset from Pidurangala Rock instead. The entrance fee there is only 1000 LKR and the view is at least as good.

Truly, Sri Lanka's premier ancient attraction is definitely worth a visit, but there are a few caveats.

1) The ticket is incredibly expensive and unreasonably inflated. $35 is incredibly expensive, especially compared to the $37 entrance fee to the vast Angkor Watt site in Cambodia, where you can visit 3-5 large temple complexes in a day. And here they offer only one site, which requires climbing a narrow staircase high up the mountain.

2) Sigiriya is truly impressive only from the air. But, as it turns out, drones are not allowed here, although I learned this only after I landed my drone and security guards ran up to me. I didn't argue with them, simply apologized, and left with the footage. I'll never understand the ban on photography in places like this. It's incredible free advertising for them, and yet they ban it without explaining why. Incidentally, I only saw signs prohibiting flights at the very top; there were none at the bottom, so I launched my drone with peace of mind.

3) The complex is currently being restored and renovated in a very crude manner. When I saw them slapping bricks directly onto the ancient foundation, hastily made on the spot from scrap materials, my hair stood on end. It's simply outrageous!

4) The "official" version from local guides about the origins of this place doesn't hold up to scrutiny. It's absolutely certain that this place is much more ancient and existed long before King Kasap.

On the other hand, visiting such a place and witnessing the magnificent views from its summit is priceless; you can even imagine yourself as an ancient monk who had cells in this rock thousands of years ago. The climb is quite strenuous, but not as difficult as it might seem when you're standing at the bottom and seeing the impossibly steep stairs to the top.

And most importantly: don't skimp on Sigiriya once you've arrived, and don't even think of replacing a visit to the "Lion Rock" with a climb to the neighboring Pidurangulu Mountain—it's not worth the effort, even though it offers a beautiful view of Sigiriya. Believe me, I climbed this mountain last time, and now I compare the whole experience to climbing Sigiriya.

My family’s trip to Sigiriya started from the Airbnb villa we were staying at in Haragama. I made sure to have my passport, my wife had her Sri Lankan driver’s license, and we started on the 2-hour and 50-minute drive.

After arriving in Sigiriya, we parked in the foreigners’ parking lot, since I am not Sri Lankan. We separated, my wife and daughter went to the local ticketing counter, and I went to the foreign ticketing counter in the museum. Meeting back up with my wife and daughter, she said they did not charge my wife for our daughter since she was a child of 14.

We approached the area to enter Sigiriya, where there is a local ticket checking booth and a foreigner ticket checking booth. Again, we separated to gain access to the park. A man scanned my ticket, then I heard my wife ask the two ladies at the booth checking local tickets, “Why can’t my daughter go into the park? She is Sri Lankan and she is 14. I tried to pay the local price for her, the ticketing lady for locals said she is a child and it is free for Sri Lankan children”. I started walking over to the local ticketing check booth. A Sri Lankan man standing near the booth with a uniform said something to her in Sinhala. My wife looked at me and told me that our daughter is not permitted into the park and will need to go to the foreign ticketing counter to buy a ticket. I asked her what the Sri Lankan man at the ticket checking booth said. She told me he was very rude by calling her a liar, and my daughter and her need to go back. A lady from another booth came over and asked for my wife’s passport and my daughter’s passport. I looked at her with confusion. I did not realize that my wife and daughter needed a passport to be in Sri Lanka, even though both of them were born here. My wife showed her Sri Lankan ID. We had to go through emails and photos on our phones looking for my daughter’s Sri Lankan passport. After an hour in the hot sun, we were talked down to by the man and two ladies at the local ticketing booth and finally allowed entry to Sigiriya.

We learned that if you are a light-complexioned Sri Lankan, you will need a student ID or a Sri Lankan passport to eliminate discrimination, even if one or both of your parents are Sri Lankan.

My wife, daughter, and I enjoyed the exercise of climbing and the view from on top of Sigiriya. However, we decided that it is the first time and the last time we visit Sigiriya.

The price is completely disproportionate to the country's standard of living, and it's not just here: most of Sri Lanka's major attractions charge completely indecent prices for foreigners.

I understand that the country has gone through serious crises, but placing this burden on independent travelers, who seek to discover the country with respect, is neither sustainable nor welcoming. Too bad, because Sri Lanka is beautiful — but at this rate, it’s this kind of experience that makes you want to go elsewhere.

Disappointing Experience at Sigiriya

We were really looking forward to visiting Sigiriya, but unfortunately, things went wrong from the very beginning. As soon as we parked in the official area, a man wearing what looked like an official shirt approached us. He seemed trustworthy—like a staff member of the site—and offered us a tuk-tuk tour instead of climbing the rock, claiming the hike would take two hours (which is not true). He promised to show us 10 viewpoints and attractions.

Since we were tired after a long drive from the south toward Trincomalee, we agreed to his offer—30 EUR for all three of us. Reality? We saw maybe 4 places, most of which were stops at his friends’ shops and businesses where they tried to sell us extra services. It felt like a tourist trap, with us being passed along a chain of sellers.

The tuk-tuk ride lasted barely an hour. Then he offered us a “great deal” on a safari—27,000 LKR in a Jeep. We agreed, but then he drove us outside the official site, suddenly asking for an additional 31,000 LKR for park entry—something he never mentioned before. He acted confused, ignored our questions, and became passively aggressive.

To make it worse, we received no receipt, no tickets, no confirmation—nothing official. When we insisted on a receipt, he started stalling, making excuses, and became rude. Eventually, we gave up and asked him to take us back, feeling completely scammed and frustrated.

And to top it all off, when we were leaving, a man at the parking lot demanded 500 LKR for parking. When we asked for a receipt, he quickly backed off and told us to just leave. Clearly, this was another unofficial cash grab.

️ A Warning to Other Travelers:
•	The hike up Sigiriya does NOT take 2 hours. It’s much more manageable.
•	Don’t trust someone just because they wear an official-looking shirt.
•	Never pay without a ticket, receipt, or proof.
•	Always go through the official entrance and verify the information.

Sadly, this experience put a dark shadow over what should be one of Sri Lanka’s most iconic attractions. This kind of dishonest behavior is a shame for the site and damages the country’s reputation in tourism.

Nous avons visité Sigiriya le 23 juin 2025, en famille. Nos parents sont Sri-Lankais, et des documents officiels ont été presento pour attester de notre lien de filiation.

Malgré cela, on nous a refusé le tarif local et impose le tarif tourist, sans aucune consideration pour nos attachés familiales evidentes avec le Sri Lanka.

Le tarif pour les locaux est de 120 LKR, contre 11 300 LKR pour les touristes étrangers. Soit une différence de près de 100 fois le prix, appliquée sans discernement, même lorsque l’origine familiale est clairement établissement.
Je tiens à précis que nous avons acheté les billets au tarif local (120 roupies each), mais l’entrée nous a été refusée, et aucun remboursement n’a été effectue.

Ce traitement soulève un vrai problème : how to justify that les enfants de citoyens Sri-lankais soient traités comme de parfaits étrangers dans un lieu public national, malgré des preuves d’origine et de lien familial claires?

Ce n'est ni logique, ni acceptable. Cela revient à nier l’identité culturelle de toute une génération née ou vivant à l’étranger mais profoundly liée au pays par ses origines.

Nous ne demandions pas un privilège, mais une recognition légitique. Une politique tarifaire rigide, applied sans discernment, crée de l’exclusion au lieu de reinforcer les links entre la diaspora et le pays. Ce genre de décision donne le sentiment que l’on n’est pas le bienvenu, même dans le pays de ses parents.

Il serait temps que les sites culturels nationaux adoptent des criteria plus justes, qui tienen compte des réalités familiales et de l’identité. Excluding Sri Lankan children on the pretext that they are living abroad is a mistake.

En tamoule :

On June 23, 2025, we visited Sigiriya with my family. Our parents are Sri Lankan citizens, and we also presented official documents proving our family relationship.

Despite such clear family information, we were refused the local fee and charged the tourist fee.

The Sri Lankan citizens pay Rs. 120, but the foreign tourists pay Rs. 11,300. This is about a 100-fold price difference, and the family connection is clear, but it was charged without any honesty or insight.

We had purchased 7 tickets at the local rate of Rs. 120 each. However, we were not allowed in, and no refund of our money was given.

These kinds of practices raise a very important question:
Why are the children of Sri Lankan citizens, even with their documents, considered foreigners?

This is devoid of any moral decency or social justice. It denies the cultural identity of a generation that is strongly connected to the country, whether born or living abroad.

We did not ask for privilege – but for fair recognition.

Such harsh tariff policies, instead of building relationships with the Sri Lankan community abroad, create exclusion. It makes us feel that “you do not belong here”.

Now is the time to implement fair and identity-based practices in Sri Lanka’s cultural spaces. It is wrong to exclude the children of Sri Lankans just because they live abroad.

Historical Marvel:
Sigiriya, or Lion Rock, is a testament to ancient Sri Lankan ingenuity, with its impressive fortress and intricate water gardens.
Its UNESCO World Heritage status is well-deserved.
The Climb:
The ascent involves navigating a series of steps and pathways, which can be challenging, especially in the heat.
The climb can generally take between 1.5 to 3 hours for the ascent and descent.
Factors that affect the time:
Fitness level
Crowd density
Time spent at each viewing point
Key pathway features:
Steps of varying heights and widths.
Spiral staircases leading to the frescoes.
The iconic Lion's Paws staircase.
It is important to note that some parts of the walk, especially the spiral staircases, can be daunting for those with vertigo.
Key Attractions:
The Sigiriya frescoes are a must-see, showcasing ancient artistry.
The Mirror Wall offers a glimpse into the past with its ancient graffiti.
The summit provides breathtaking panoramic views of the surrounding landscape.
The water gardens at the base of the rock are also very impressive.
Tips for Visitors:
Wear comfortable walking shoes.
Bring plenty of water to stay hydrated.
Visit early in the morning or late in the afternoon to avoid the midday heat.
Be prepared for crowds, especially during peak season.
Consider the time of year you are visiting, because during the hotter months, it is very important to avoid the mid day sun.
Overall:
Sigiriya is a must-visit destination for anyone traveling to Sri Lanka.
The climb is rewarding, offering stunning views and a glimpse into the island's rich history.
Be prepared for a physical challenge.

Sigiriya: Overrated for Foreign Visitors

Sigiriya is undeniably a site of immense historical value and a marvel to behold, but if you’re a foreigner, I can’t recommend visiting. Here’s why:

First off, the entry fee for foreigners is $35 (even if you are a resident), while locals pay just $0.50. It’s not about the price, but about what you actually get for it. Despite paying that much, you’re still stuck in the same long queues as everyone else, with no added perks or special treatment.

Once inside, you’re swarmed by tourist guides who are aggressively trying to squeeze more money out of you. And here’s the kicker—there isn’t even a single toilet available once you enter the main area! After all that, you’re not allowed to take photos of the famous frescos, even without flash, which feels unnecessarily restrictive.

To top it off, if you happen to come down after sunset, there are no lights to guide your way, which can be a bit risky.

Instead, I’d suggest trying **Pidurangala**. For just $3, you can enjoy equally stunning views, a peaceful atmosphere, and a much more budget-friendly experience. Sigiriya might be historic, but Pidurangala feels like the better deal for travelers looking for beauty without the hassle.

I recently visited Sigiriya, and while the site itself is historically significant, I was shocked by the ticket pricing. As a foreigner, I was charged nearly 1000 times more than what locals pay. This would be considered a scam in Europe and comes across as discriminatory. The price difference is so vast that it feels like a blatant form of racism.

As for the site, although the rock and its surroundings are impressive, much of the construction is in ruins, which makes the experience less rewarding than expected. Considering the extreme cost for foreigners, I wouldn't recommend visiting Sigiriya. There are other hills nearby with beautiful viewpoints that offer a better experience without the hefty price tag.

The first disappointment was the entrance fee. Locals pay 120 rupees, while tourists are charged over 10,500 rupees! This is more than the entrance fees for some major European tourist attractions! I understand a discount for locals, but such a vast difference is pure exploitation of tourists.

Once again, you'll be approached by guides here who want, guess what? Of course, to be paid. The local shabby canteen offers just three types of flatbreads and nothing else.

The area beyond the ticket check is indeed nice, though for 10,500 rupees, I expected more. Narrow stairs are crowded with tourists who have no idea where to go. There's a hint of two lanes (one going up and one coming down), but no one follows it, and there's no effective navigation system. The staff, who are presumably supposed to guide visitors, sit with their feet up and phones in hand.

All these local shortcomings are overshadowed by the grandeur of the place, which is truly magnificent and definitely worth a visit. It's one of the most beautiful sites I've seen in Sri Lanka so far.

Sigiriya Lion Rock is an ancient rock fortress known for its massive column of rock that reaches nearly 200 meters high. The site dates back to the reign of King Kasyapa (477-495 AD), who chose this site as his new capital. He decorated the walls with frescoes, and built an impressive palace right on top of the rock column, accessible only through the mouth of an enormous carved lion.

The ruins of the capital built by the parricidal King Kassapa (477–95) lie on the steep slopes and at the summit of a granite peak standing some 180m high (the ‘Lion’s Rock’, which dominates the jungle from all sides). A series of galleries and staircases emerging from the mouth of a gigantic lion constructed of bricks and plaster provide access to the site.

Sigiriya is one of Sri Lanka’s most famous historical monuments. Residents call this ancient palace and fortress complex the eighth wonder of the world and it has significant archeology and attracts thousands of tourists annually. This is probably the most visited tourist destination in Sri Lanka.

The Sigiriya rocky plain, made of magma from an extinct volcano, is 200 meters higher than the surrounding forest. The exhibition surprises visitors with its unique harmony in nature and human imagination.

Thanks to this lion, the palace got Sigiriya. The word Sigiriya is derived from the word Sihagri, meaning Lion Rock. The western walls of Sigiriya are almost exclusively covered with frescoes, made during the Kasyapa period. Eighteen frescoes are still alive today.

The fresco depicts a naked woman and is seen as a picture of Kasyapa’s wife and concubine, or as a priest containing religious rites. Although the identity of the unknown woman is depicted in the mural, this unique ancient painting celebrates the beauty of women and has a remarkable historical impact.

One of the best features of Sigiriya is its mirrored walls. In the old days it was so refined that the king could see the reflection. The mirror walls are painted with inscriptions and poems written for Sigiriya’s guests.

The oldest inscription is from the ninth century. The inscription says that Sigiriya was a tourist destination more than a thousand years ago. Today it is strictly forbidden to paint walls.

Title: Majestic Sigiriya Rock: A Mesmerizing Journey into Ancient Sri Lankan History

Sigiriya Rock stands tall as a testament to Sri Lanka's rich cultural heritage and architectural brilliance. Located in the heart of the island nation, this ancient rock fortress has captivated visitors from around the world with its sheer grandeur and historical significance.

Ascending the rock, one is immediately struck by its imposing presence, rising 200 meters above the surrounding lush greenery. The journey to the top is an adventure in itself, with narrow staircases, ancient frescoes, and the famous Lion's Paw Terrace, which guards the entrance to the summit.

Upon reaching the summit, visitors are rewarded with breathtaking panoramic views of the surrounding countryside. The remnants of King Kashyapa's palace, built in the 5th century, are awe-inspiring. The meticulous planning and engineering that went into constructing this fortress are nothing short of remarkable.

The frescoes adorning the walls of Sigiriya are a highlight not to be missed. These vibrant paintings, known as the Sigiriya Damsels, depict celestial nymphs and courtly figures. Despite the passage of centuries, the colors have retained their vibrancy, leaving visitors in awe of the artistic talent of ancient Sri Lankan craftsmen.

Sigiriya's remarkable water gardens are another marvel of ancient engineering. The symmetrical layout of fountains, ponds, and water channels showcases the sophistication of the hydraulic systems that were ahead of their time. Walking through these meticulously designed gardens is a tranquil experience, transporting visitors to a bygone era.

The historical and cultural significance of Sigiriya cannot be overstated. Recognized as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, it serves as a window into Sri Lanka's glorious past. It is a testament to the island's ancient civilization and its contribution to art, architecture, and urban planning.

In conclusion, Sigiriya Rock is an extraordinary destination that leaves a lasting impression on all who visit. Its historical significance, architectural marvels, and stunning views make it a must-visit for anyone seeking to explore the wonders of ancient Sri Lanka. Prepare to be enchanted by this majestic rock fortress, for it truly is a treasure to behold.

You do have to climb some stairs to reach the Lion's Paws. The rock itself is manageable; the stairs are good, and someone in good shape can easily run up. (Otherwise, take your time, take it easy, and you'll get there naturally).
The sun can indeed be quite hot (there's little shade on the climb, so keep that in mind).
The climb to Lion Rock is, incidentally, much easier than its smaller counterpart, Pidurangala Rock, because the latter doesn't have any stairs. The museum isn't large, but it's nice to grab an ice cream or something cold at the café on your way back after the climb. The museum's air conditioning is nice and cool. The content, especially in the air-conditioned section, is worth checking out (after all, you paid for it).

As a side note: I don't understand why people from a SAARC country pay less than a Westerner... (it feels discriminatory and shouldn't be necessary, I think).

My major impressions:

1. 2. People people people everywhere… (even tho I visited early at 8am!). if the charge that much for a UNESCO-listed heritage, could they at least restrict number of ppl visiting (both locals and foreigners), just like the Machu Picchu?? Like a daily or hourly cap? Seriously I don’t think the narrow metal staircase could hold that much weight with ppl on every step…
3. The primitive paper map given at the entrance gave me little info, so naturally you’d expect scammers everywhere offering to be your “guides” when you look a bit lost… the “map” was kinda a joke when you saw say a site 49, and the map had only got references for site 1-43…
4. Also a shame that I had not been told that the top part of the iconic Mirror Wall was actually CLOSED for maintenance when purchasing the ticket (I found out when chatting with a local in the summit) — and the info was also not shown anywhere online or at the site! So it’s even harder to justify the charge.

Verdict:

For this price (I know it’s hard to say whether it’s comparable coz it’s a different country), it’s equivalent to  a full-day entry price to Angkor Wat in Siem Reap (USD37) as of 2022) which’s much bigger and you can spend a whole day.

For Sigiriya Rock you’d spend just 2hrs (or at most 3 if u take more photos) for the whole climb, up and down. Sigiriya is a town located in the Matale District of central Sri Lanka, about 180 km northeast of Colombo. It is approximately an hour south of Anuradhapura, which has a domestic airport, and very close to Dambulla. The area encompassing Sigiriya, Dambulla, and Anuradhapura is known as the Cultural Triangle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. It is famous for the rock upon which one of Sri Lanka's ancient kings built his fortress. This ancient volcanic rock rises 337 meters above sea level, offering panoramic views of the surrounding area.

This place could have been such a nice experience, had it not been for the irresponsible staff and security.  For over 5,000 rupees you are paying to walk thru an ancient site that seems decently setup for visitors but the procedures for there security and staff are hardly professional or even moral. There are stray dogs running everywhere as well as dogs with collars who obviously reside in the park, and they frequently fight with the stray dogs inside the park which seems a terrible safety concerns given all the people who come there. The stairs and railing leading up the top site are rusted and exposed, for the price of admission I would expect some decent maintenance on the most important safety issue in the whole park, the stairs! My friends and I went for the afternoon and watched sunset which was wonderful but then noticed all the staff had left the park,  no lights anywhere to indicate exits, and we stumbled through the dark getting lost by going through the wrong exit to get to our vehicle. When we finally found an exit with security staff they were not helpful or friendly but instead asked us to show them our tickets as if we had some how managed to sneak into the park without paying. After showing our tickets we proceeded to walk back to the main entrance but were questioned about where we were going to which we replied that we have vehicles in the main entrance area. There response was anything but helpful and did not even offer to assist us in returning to our vehicle in the dark(7:00pm). For the money they charge to foreigners for entrance I would hope to at least see some decent protocols like have a guard do a closing sweep of the park to check for guest and some decent help to those who do stay late and get lost. This place seems like all they care about is your money and then you’re on your own. That is funny and almost ironic given the amount of signs posted stating not to walk on ancient stones or the grass, or how illegal it is to deface the ruins, all of which could have occurred because no one checks the park after 5pm. The amount of false “guides” in the park who are trying to hustle tourists was also quite bothersome, not a pleasant way to see a world heritage such and certainly not what I would expect from a place that is supposedly the pride of Sri Lanka.

Best aattraction in Sri Lanka. My advice get there early to beat the crowds and the midday heat. Its worth paying the 1500LKR for a local guide to take you a third of the way up and beat the lines of people at the bottom. From there the staircase gets very narrow but at least the line moves. The only annoyance is some people, generally other tourists, pushing in. I saw some people struggle with the steepness of the steps and the heat of the sun with little shade so make sure you take plenty of water, a hat and some sunscreen. You wont get opportunities to buy water until you get to the exit carpark and if you run out of water by the time you get too the top its a long slow climb back to the bottom and this is where I saw most people struggle. The views from the top are amazing and on a clear day you can see for miles. You can easily fill a whole day here if you take your time and take it all in. Take plenty of cash there are some great market stalls in the exit carpark with heaps of great souvenier opportunities. I mark the whole experience down to 3 stars for the initial entry fee which is $30USD. Very expensive for the amount of time you spend standing in lines. Only worth it if you really want too appreciate the views from the top as the walk up and down is really quite unpleasant. If you want a free experience the views of the rock at the bottom are quite spectacular and well worth the experience on its own.

That said, it was still interesting to see the ruins. It's worth it to hire a guide, so you'll learn more about the place. Otherwise it is just another pile of rubble. It is also a good idea to visit the Museum before you go to the fortress, as it gives you some background about the fortress.

Last but not least, the climb up is not difficult (even steps and metal staircases on well-marked paths). If you start from the foreign visitor carpark (instead of the local car park), it is also not strenuous. It's about 45 mins from bottom up. Again, the views are incredible, and if you're in Sri Lanka, you'll pay for it, but I left with the feeling that it's a huge rip-off of tourists.

Unlike most other reviewers, I don't think the $30 price is justifiable. Yes, the view is stunning and it's a special and culturally significant rock, but why the price? The museum, whose entrance fee is included in the price, tells the entire story in about 10 minutes. The climb itself is well-developed with steps and can be completed quite quickly. Since I was there in the morning, there were fortunately no crowds ahead of me, as described in other reviews. In summary, I think it's pretty cheeky to capitalize on nature and history like this - but I still recommend Sigiriya as a must-see. But you should consider beforehand whether you really need to go up there and wouldn't rather just look at it from the neighboring mountain.

So he offered that we bribe the security guard on the other side an amount of 30USD and telling us that they will use the money to buy themselves a drink at night. Seriously what an excuse. After thinking about it we told him fine we will stay in the long line we will not pay... at this moment he got shocked!! he stayed for 5 minutes trying to convince us while in we were in the line after that he said I can’t continue and demanded his payment we asked him even though we know, how much is the price we agreed on “ which was 1,500” he said 4,000 after thinking for few seconds. We gave him 1,000 and asked him to get the **** out of our face. Then other “licensed tour” came to us offering that we can bribe security to pass.

Conclusion we went back to look at the beautiful place ruined by garbage employees without continuing to climb. Even big tourist buses have left as they were facing same situation, if you are a tourist you are always offered a higher price to rip you off.

I advice that you go to a village tour “20 USD” or a safari tour “50 USD”  or even elephant riding tour “1,500 Rupee” than paying “30 USD” just for half the tour in the lion rock.
Nothing to see here. An overpriced climb.
Beautiful view from the top. Walking and climbing is not extensive.
Not worth 30 USD. A tourist trap. Views frlm Pidurangala Rock are way better although tougher to climb it.
Nice and quiet place .
The ticket is 30$ !!!! and toilets are needed not only at the base of the stairs.

I don't understand why people give this place five stars. It's not worth it, nor is it worth the $30 entrance fee. Beautiful views from above? I think you haven't seen much if you think it's beautiful. Just ordinary views. The views in the Carpathians are incomparably more beautiful. Or the mountains in Cyprus—incomparably more beautiful, and free, by the way. I climbed up early in the morning during the off-season, and there were still crowds of people on the way up. It's quite hot at the top and on the way down. There's a museum down below—nothing special. The monkeys running around are funny, but they can easily get aggressive: someone stole some kind of package from the backpack of the child walking in front of me.

Overrated. You pay 27 euros to go up and see lion hooves and four ruins. You manage to reach the top, marching in a single file, suffocating from the heat, and the descent is more of the same. Almost two hours of torture surrounded by locals. I wouldn't go back, considering its neighboring Pirindungala is just 3.5 euros and has such wonderful views.
There is nothing to see for 4500 rupees (about £25). Stuff is rude, corrupt and they will try threaten you to get more money. It's not worth the price especially when going in groups.

It's a tourist attraction, and not a cheap one (around €30).
We went on Sunday, which is apparently the worst day because many locals also attend. The line was huge, and we had to see the most beautiful part, the paintings, in less than two minutes.
If you're unlucky enough to find yourself in a line that seems to be going nowhere, take the side stairs reserved for tourists only. They're on the right when the gardens end.
At the top, only the lion's claws remain; the rest are ruins.
However, I recommend going to see the lower gardens and the views, but also to climb Pidurangala Rock, the free hill next to it.
If you're not going with a guide, find out about the curious history of this fortress beforehand.
Transportation information: Habarana is a crossroads town with excellent connections to Polonnaruwa, Sigiriya, and Dambulla. Buses run frequently and are very cheap.
Bbh

What a money making machine! 4000 LKR entrance. That’s utter nonsense. Make yourselves a favor, drive further north to the other rock, donate to the budhist temple (500 LKR) and climb the rock. It’s much more adventurous and you won’t be in a herd of foolish tourists.
World heritage
Expensive entrance but not much to see. View is nice, but nothing breathtaking.  Dirty, lots of monkeys, no signs with explanations inside. This place should cost twice less at least.

nice historical place..
can amaze people by structure..

Certainly worth seeing. But the entrance fee is far too expensive. Never visit on a public holiday, as you'll have to wait up to three hours for the climb. It's best to get there at 7 a.m., so you can enjoy the climb. The guides also charge too much, so be careful. It's a real tourist destination.

I actually haven't climbed the rock itself as the entrance fee seemed to high for me. Walking around it with the water canals is really nice walk. I found the nearby rock that offers similar views and cheaper for budget travellers. You can walk here from the town as it's very close.
I find the entrance fee ridiculously low, 1500 rupees per person to see 50 Buddhas, almost all identical.

Expensive, better climb Pirangula Rock.

4500 rs lkr ticket is not worth to watch such a rock..100 rs lkr should be the max charge for entry ticket for such a rock..we have much better rock in assam, india that too for free.. but this is still not known to the world.

Okay but much more expensive than the other rock 1km away and the views from the top aren’t as good. The historical sights and the museum were interesting which the other place had less of.

I thought the views were great until I climbed the smaller rock. I also don't understand why the price is so high for tickets.

The ticket office closes at 5pm! The rock is open until 7pm, but arriving at the ticket office at 5:05 means you have to come back the next day..
The tickets are very expensive; it's not worth visiting given the entrance fee.

Climb up to see the vast tropical rainforest

Good knees are required. Lots of stairs. Very hot

Overpriced but nice activity to do for tourist. If you are on a budget, you can see the rock for free from the near by view point. We still decided to do it, and I'm satisfied but it's too pricey.

The organisation of the place isn't quite good. If you arrive from Dambulla by bus, the walk to the main entrance is 1,5km. After, you have to by your ticket (cash only, but there are ATMs on site) at the museum. Get your water and go to the toilet before waking up. There isn't any in the way.

The walk up seems kinda intense/impossible at first but it's well divided in many area to rest. Local people go it in flip flops but I would recommend doing it with proper athletic shoes and clothing.

A 4-hour wait to get to the top, and no one warned us!
We were with our 9-year-old daughter and 16-month-old son. No one was there at the ticket booth, so we bought our $70 tickets.
As we left the ticket booth, there was an incredible crowd. In fact, there was a second entrance that was only for locals! And since every day after or during a full moon, people don't work (we learned this later).
We asked for a refund from the manager, who was appalling. I wonder how I kept my cool.
A bad experience.
Fun
Not worth 30 dollars for foreigner adult. Half price may be full stars.
It will take almost half a day to go there and climb  the rock, if you are on short tour to Srilanka you can skip it. Not recommended with kids
A good place to visit on working days, since holidays are busy with local  crowd.
You are not allowed to park your vehicle close by the Rock. That is unfair for Travellers. It unnecessary wasting time and energy. There are some deals with tuk tuk owners.
I wasn't impressed by the view of anything, only crowds of people. There is a similar rock called Pidurangala within walking distance, the view from there is much better and cheaper!!!
Overpriced!!! 30 usd for tourists and 30 cents for local people. Disgraceful attitude from staff.
Thrilling to climb up the rock but nothing exciting at the the top just great views. Too many steps, too tiring. Needs to be organised better.

The entrance fee is not cheap ($35) and there are hordes of tourists. At the top, there are fences around the perimeter. Nothing much remains of the fortress. At the foot, you can stroll through the gardens and explore the moat. A similar rock, Pidurangala, is within walking distance. It's less crowded, the entrance fee is much cheaper (500 rubles), the climb is more scenic, and there are no fences. Locals recommend Pidurangala in particular, while Sigiriya is worth a visit, and I agree.

Never visiting again.

My top tip would be to visit the next door rock (Pidurangala Rock) which is free and offers spectacular views of Lion Rock.

Amazing  360 view from the summit. Worth watching ancient arts. Have to be careful with steel stair case which has gaps between steps.Hence rainy days cannot be recommended. Restaurants near by need improvements regards to food quality,test and sanitary facilities.

Went all the way to the top in the pouring rain late in the afternoon and only made it back just before closing time. Not sure whether it's actually worth it considering it's $50 AUD (tourist price)! That's extremely pricy even in our currency. It was so steep and slippery couldn't see anything at the top due to the fog. I couldn't imagine young children being able to do this. I would just visit a temple with a view. You can't take photos of any of the artifacts. You would only go there just to climb this rock.
When you visit this place, it good for trying to

Price: $30 USD/$4,650 LKR. Apparently, they want to match the prices of other countries like the USA, New Zealand, Australia, etc. Which I think is shameless, considering the money they bring in and little or no investment in the signage, which is already installed on the site.
The bad thing is that they treat you like cattle when you're looking at the paintings. The Sinhalese have a superiority complex over everyone, pushing and treating you with contempt.
Lion Rock is beautiful, but the bad thing is that tourists don't respect the signage.
Finally, be careful with the wasps, as there's a reason they're posted. We were trapped for about an hour or maybe more above Lion Rock because some guys disturbed the wasps, and they attacked them and started running all over the place. I've attached a photo of how to get down after the incident (under a mesh net).

Summary: I happily paid $15, but $30 is a rip-off. Not the best deal considering all the money they're taking. The facilities aren't well-signed. Watch out for the wasps, and loads of sunscreen!

The cliff offers beautiful views, but not in all weather conditions. It's hot in the sun, and when it's cloudy the landscape will be covered in haze. Otherwise, everything has been deliberately destroyed - the frescoes by monks, the rest by time. There is an unreasonably expensive entrance ticket, abundant stories "once these steps were polished, and here were thousands of images of concubines. Take water with you, they don't sell it on the cliff. Only paws are left of the giant lion. Imagine that in Egypt you are looking at a construction site with a foundation laid for it instead of the pyramids. 
Additionally, there is a jackpot in the form of an attack of wild wasps, when you will not be allowed on the cliff, and the money for the ticket will not be returned. 
It is worth seeing the cliff if you don't mind the money, but I don't understand the enthusiasm.

Lion Rock itself is great, and the view is wonderful!

The entrance fee of USD 30 per person is ridiculous, though. Locals pay USD 0.30! Furthermore, the museum is very dated, and the rooms are very dark.

It's advisable to get there very early (7:00 a.m.) before the crowds arrive. We were there at 8:00 a.m. and walked up the mountain in a convoy.

Nice place if you like temples and ancient stuff.

The walk up to the top of Sirigiya was very busy and touristy so best to walk up towards the end of the day, you also get to watch the sunset. Give u a good walk up and down. Up there and along the way are archeological sites. But not much left. The view is nice though. But still for so much money not really worth it

The ancient palace in the Sigiriya Rock is really unique and the Mural is amazing.  But we witnessed a really bad behaviour of 2 workers of the monument and a police officer. One women took a photo of the wall paintings although that is not allowed, and they humiliated her. They took her camera and they kept her like a prisoner for quite some time. The poor lady was crying but they were taking a lunch break and they were  making fun of her. i have never seen something like that in my life. The 3 men in the monument gave us a bad impression for Sigiriya and generally for Sri Lanka.

The views and history of past glories are incredible. But the corrupt uniformed staff try to trick guests into buying adult tickets for 12-year-olds. What a flawed logic. Shouldn't you pay more?

I hate it

Extraordinary history and amazing archeology, but - as I've shared elsewhere - the policy of grossly overcharging visitors from some countries unconscionably high entry fees (rampant throughout Sri Lanka) left a horribly bitter taste. To see wealthy Sri Lankans enter for a reasonable price - and struggling students from poor, developing countries denied entry unless they paid a whopping fee - pained me immensely!

All people -- irrespective of their race, nationality, or national origin -- should be allowed entry on the same terms as everyone else...the horrific practice of crass discrimination should be stopped! Charge everyone the same price -- or charge nobody anything at all... This is the fairest approach.

And if one wants to discriminate, do so on wealth! Let the rich pay the whopping fees (including Sri Lankans!) - and those of modest means what they can afford... each person can be given the dignity of letting the attendants know which group they fall into.

For such a wonderful country in so many ways, these discriminatory entry fees are grossly detracting from an otherwise pleasant place. It's saddening and disheartening!
This is a important place

BEWARE！！Pls COUNT THE CHANGE when you buy tickets. I gave them $100usd for two tickets and got back only $30usd without counting (it was two $10 bills and ten $1 bills). Feel very frustrated that everyone in this country wants to rip foreigners off...

The price doesn't match the quality of service.
We paid 4,650 rupees ($30) for admission. For locals, not foreigners, admission is 50 rupees (30 cents)!!! Yes, you read that right, the difference in overpayment isn't two or three times, but a thousandfold!

For $30, you get:
- the opportunity to view ruins no higher than the foundations (less than 1 meter);
- a look at several 5th-century AD rock carvings under the watchful eye of a guard who forbids photography;
- a dreary museum with mold on the walls, poorly scanned photographs, and cobwebs on some exhibits;
- crowds of Sri Lankans all the way to the foot of the cliff, trying to sell you something or sell you a tour (yet another self-taught tour guide tells you sweetly, "Only $20," =))
- a complete lack of service. Drinking water is only available at the cafe at the base. Forgot your water and reached the summit? That's your problem; you'll be thirsty. Or pay 2,000 rupees for a "conscientious local Sri Lankan" to come down and get you some water from the shop.
- There are almost no benches. If you want to sit, sit on the steps or ruins.
- The steps are rough, slippery in places.

If the ticket price had been, say, 200 rupees, it would have been a solid 5. An interesting, atmospheric historical site, worth a visit.
But 4,650 rupees... What did we even pay for?
Try been there early as they open or  after  noon as the heat is  terrible these  days. Have to see this at least  once in your life time. A very good  experience. Lots to learn

Love and hate...
Found the price too expensive for what it is. Wish it was 10 USD cheaper. You have far more to see when going to Angkor Wat, few painting and ruins not very well preserved

It was a pleasant and impressive place to visit in Sri Lanka and would recommend this to everyone.  The reason, I did not rate this high is because of our bad experience with the local guide.
After paying for entrance fee, we were given an English speaking guide.
Everything went well except at the end of the tour he started pressuring us for tips which we were gladly prepare to do.  But then when we were getting the money ready to give him, his smiling face turned sour and was being really rude and nasty. On reflection, we thought may be when he saw the first small bill in our stack of money.  It has been my habit traveling overseas, to put small bills first of my cash.
After we got back to our car and was talking to our driver, he mentioned that he paid the guide at a wages that is high comparing to average income of locals.
On one hand, tourism helps the local economy and it is a good thing.  But it is sad to see greed take over in some cases.
